author: @AlanFrance21
text: Arthur Paul Pedrick, [Google him], a retired UK Patent Office clerk, was responsible for some of the maddest UK patents ever issued, from rolling giant snowballs from the Arctic for fresh water supplies, to a large canon mounted on a pith helmet for shooting over the top of trenches.  In some of his patents, he named his pet cat Ginger as a technical assistant!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - i did google him - clever guy!

replies.author: @AlanFrance21
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Apologies.  That should of course have read "cannon", to clarify the issue, in case some dear reader imagined a beaming Rt. Revd. hurtling over no mans land giving blessings to all below!

author: @das250250
text:
I think this is a great idea Rob . Only last week I was pondering having a community of independents mainly from a physics perspective . It seems very few scientists are able able to free tank ideas and pursue them due to the structure of modern day research funding . Richard Feynman in an interview discussed that if he had been offered to refuse Nobel prize he would have because countless times it interrupted his work . Not only but he mentions that the boards he was asked to be on we're more concerned with who should be future members than actually attending to how to help solve the current problems needing solution. He left most of these positions because of this dogma . 
Another is a British scientist studying LSD for depression stated that he could not publish his work because of the pressure from pharma companies holding a strangle hold on scientists who could publish in USA . 
The technologies are starting to form for this opportunity ..
Very exciting times ahead .

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am actually mulling this over as to how to go about doing it - I am thinking of a not for profit with a board and journal

replies.author: @das250250
replies.text: Yes I have some stuff I could share regarding this .. It's been swimming in my head over the last week.

replies.author: @cheerdiver
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Will Ben Krasnow (Applied Science), have GOD status?  If so, I'm in would love to pick his brain.
Think there are some "sci-fi" devices that need to be disclosed the public, like using vibrations to overcome masonry tensile strength.  A "sonic drill".

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
cheerdiver
Ben is a bit of a genius - but i don't think he even watches my channel lol

author: @joeglory425
text: I like how the internet has made access to information so much easier.  It enables an independence that allows diverse topics to be researched and then experimented with from home. 20 years ago I could only get similar information from a college library.  So now, not just the people at Universities can be scientists!

author: @cyprianserapion2022
text:
Independence is only given if this is financially independence exists in this world where money is the idol of all worship.
The idea is good but when we finally get away to set off everything with money.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I can't tell you how much I disagree with that - It reads as a recipe for doing nothing. I know tons of people who research while holding down a day job - it's not money but attitude and the attitude you expound isn't the one to have if you want to do something

replies.author: @cyprianserapion2022
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
No, it's not the recipe for doing nothing, it is the recipe to be vigilant and not to be corrupted by money.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Michael Zieger
I misunderstood - sorry about that and fair comment.

replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
My mistake, I should express myself more precisely. ;-)

author: @AaronHarper
text: Where do I sign? :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @AaronHarper
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Ah, so I am!  Lost my certificate and card in the great lab accident last year.  In other news, my lab assistant will be fine, though the feathers she has sprouted show a rather attractive iridescence. :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Aaron Harper
lol - must re-issue - actually i was thinking of a logo - something gothic i think lol

replies.author: @AaronHarper
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Awesome.  Perhaps even a silk screened lab coat like these?  https://www.sparkfun.com/products/retired/9790.

In all seriousness, lab accidents have occurred, and we have developed some protocols.  Much of this is a work in progress (the signage particularly so), but the levels and precautions have served us well for years.  Have a look if you have time, and please comment!  https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Eh9XaCr3Q2aD5MSZoYODjSA4cS7VTsvloGi7qO-U-jc/edit?usp=sharing

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Aaron Harper
I like the labcoat lol - My wife is a teacher as it happens and is involved in risk assessment all the time. I am fortunate enough to work on my own quite a bit of the time so most of my assessments are 'me-centric' but I do a fair bit of consultancy work for different companies around the world and I have found concepts of risk to be associated with locale more often than not. I think there is an element of education in risk which is sensible, right and proper and an awareness of risk is certainly raised with the document you have and to a degree being more aware is a good thing - I was going to continue with this then I realized it was going to be a very long reply - so - i'll stop here and do a vid lol
